subject: Inference: Extend default cuda-graph coverage to 512 tokens (#5797)

findings.claim: The diff introduces `cuda_graph_max_tokens` with default 512 and changes the *default* prefill/mixed CUDA-graph token ceiling from `max_requests * (num_speculative_tokens + 1)` (previously the same tight decode bound) to `min(max(512, decode_bound), max_tokens)` in dynamic_context.py:670-673. Whether this helps or hurts is deployment-dependent: for configs where `max_requests * (num_spec+1) > 512` (e.g. max_requests=64 -> 64, fine; but max_requests=1024 -> 1024 > 512), the new default *narrows* prefill/mixed graph coverage down to 512, since the clamp is `min(..., max_tokens)` but the value fed is 512 which can be below the previous decode-derived bound only when decode_bound>512 — in that case max(512,decode_bound)=decode_bound preserves it. Conversely for small max_requests it *widens* coverage (more/larger graphs captured up to 512). The real regression risk: capturing graphs up to 512 tokens by default increases warmup/capture time and CUDA-graph memory footprint for every deployment that previously used the tight decode bound (<512). This is the classic warmup-shape/coverage tradeoff the historical cases flag.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine warmup/create_cuda_graphs time and peak CUDA-graph memory for a decode-heavy config (small max_requests, e.g. 16-64) before vs after this change; the new default 512 ceiling captures additional/larger prefill-mixed graphs that were previously bounded by the decode limit.

subject: Document stacked dependent PR handling in split PR skill (#5496)
Update mcore skill owners (#5586)
Delegate reasoning token retention to the chat template in multi-turn conversations (#5276)

findings.claim: In megatron/rl/inference/megatron.py::base_generate (the per-request hot path for RL rollouts), the extra_body now unconditionally sets "return_raw_text": True. This flips on the server-side return_raw_text path in chat_completions.py, which calls tokenizer.detokenize(result["prompt_tokens"]) for EVERY completion and builds prompt_str + text_output. As the diff's own TODO comment admits, this 'wastes a lot of CPU cycles detokenizing prompt tokens, especially expensive for long prompts in agentic RL.' Notably, the parent code in the coordinator already avoided this detokenization when prompt was provided (the removed `if finished_request["prompt"] is None: detokenize(...)` block), and the parent chat_completions built raw_text from result["prompt"]+result["generated_text"] (already-detokenized strings) with no per-request re-detokenization. The new return_raw_text path re-detokenizes prompt_tokens on the hot path where the parent did not.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure chat/completions latency and coordinator CPU for RL rollouts with long prompts (e.g. 8k-token prompts) comparing return_raw_text=True vs False; assert per-request detokenize cost scales with prompt length.

subject: Fix PR template typo (#5566)
Add CI duties to oncall (#5510)
Update PR instructions (#5592)
Implement Quantile Balancing in MoE (#5349)

findings.claim: The new `quantile_balancing` routing path (router.py) issues a blocking `torch.distributed.all_gather_into_tensor(full_logits, ...)` over `tp_cp_group` on EVERY microbatch forward when `gather_size > 1` (TP/CP > 1). This all-gather transfers the whole logits tensor `[local_num_tokens * gather_size, num_experts]` and sits on the router critical path with no overlap. The existing aux-loss balancing paths only `all_reduce` small `[num_experts]` per-expert tensors on `tp_cp_group`, not a full-logits gather. This is a new per-microbatch collective serialized against router compute (a hideable stall in the finalize/routing critical path). Cost scales with global sequence length and TP*CP size.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE forward-pass latency with moe_router_load_balancing_type=quantile_balancing under TP=2/4 + CP, compared against aux_loss routing at the same config; measure router step time and the all_gather stall.

findings.claim: `qb_dual_update` performs a column-wise top-k over the FULL gathered token dimension: `(scores - alpha).topk(col_target + 1, dim=0)` where `col_target = num_tokens * k // num_experts`. A `dim=0` top-k across all tokens (potentially tens of thousands of rows) selecting a large k is a costly sort-like reduction executed per-microbatch on the hot routing path. This is on top of the per-token `(scores - beta).topk(k + 1, dim=1)`. There is no fast-path/gate (e.g. torch.kthvalue for the quantile boundary instead of a full top-k that materializes col_target+1 values), so QB routing is materially slower per step than the standard topk routing it parallels.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark qb_dual_update vs standard compute_topk on scores of shape [global_seq_len, num_experts] for representative num_tokens (e.g. 8192-65536) and num_experts (e.g. 64-256); isolate the dim=0 topk cost.

subject: Document agent PR commit sign-off and signing (#5381)
Narrow oncall responsibilities (#5490)
Set Bert TE spec q/k_layernorm to None (#5687)

findings.claim: The BERT TE spec change flips q_layernorm/k_layernorm from IdentityOp to None. When config.qk_layernorm=True, SelfAttention's fallback `submodules.q_layernorm or TENorm` (attention.py:1664) now instantiates a real TENorm for Q and K, adding two extra standalone LayerNorm kernels per attention layer in both forward and backward. Previously IdentityOp was a no-op. This is a per-layer op-count increase on the attention hot path whenever qk_layernorm is enabled — behavior/perf regression relative to the prior hardcoded IdentityOp. Note: the newly added test explicitly exercises this fallback (test_qk_layernorm_from_config_fallback), confirming a real TE LayerNorm is now built.
findings.suggested_benchmark: BERT TE training step time with config.qk_layernorm=True vs False, comparing extra TENorm Q/K kernels vs prior IdentityOp path.

subject: Inference: Add load aware routing to prefix caching.  (#5607)

findings.claim: The default coordinator routing policy is changed from FIRST_PREFIX_BLOCK to LOAD_BALANCED in three places simultaneously: `InferenceConfig.prefix_caching_coordinator_policy` (megatron/core/inference/config.py:302), the argparse default for `--inference-dynamic-batching-prefix-caching-coordinator-policy` (megatron/training/arguments.py:1946), and `InferenceSetupConfig` (megatron/training/config/inference_config.py:178). LOAD_BALANCED is explicitly defined to *ignore prefix affinity* ('Route to the rank with the fewest in-flight requests. Ignores prefix affinity.'). In `get_best_data_parallel_rank`, the LOAD_BALANCED branch short-circuits and returns `get_least_loaded_data_parallel_rank()` BEFORE the `enable_prefix_caching`/`_match_vector` affinity logic is ever consulted. Consequently, even when a user enables prefix caching (`--inference-dynamic-batching-prefix-caching true`) but does not explicitly override the coordinator policy, requests are no longer routed to the rank already holding the matching prefix KV blocks. Cross-request prefix KV reuse across DP ranks is silently lost by default, forcing prefixes to be recomputed on whichever rank happens to be least loaded — a throughput regression for prefix-heavy / shared-system-prompt workloads. This is the same mechanism as the historical priors: a static default value flips a performance feature off, not a runtime guard misfiring.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DP=8 coordinator with prefix caching enabled, many requests sharing a long common prompt prefix; measure cross-rank prefix cache hit rate and prefill FLOPs/latency under default policy (LOAD_BALANCED) vs FIRST_PREFIX_BLOCK vs longest_prefix.
